rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE HERMAN F. JOHNSON, SR. REVOCABLE 2010 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Expunge Notice Of Pendency
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 6] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Joseph M. Quinn. Petitioner Belinda F. Robinson and Objector Herman F. Johnson are siblings, both are children of Herman F. Johnson, Sr. (sometimes "Settlor") The latter, who died in 2010, is the settlor of the Herman F. Johnson, Sr. Revocable 2010 Trust ("Trust"). Robinson filed a Petition for Order Confirming That an Interest in the Real Property Commonly Known as 163 Beverly Street, San Francisco, California, is an Asset of the Trust to be Held, Administered and Distributed Under the Terms of the Trust ("Petition"). By the petition, Robinson seeks an order under Probate Code section 850 declaring that the real property known as 163 Beverly Street is an asset of their father's Trust. Johnson opposes the petition and contends that the Trust is invalid, and the real property is not a Trust asset, but an asset of their father's estate. Robinson filed a lis pendens on the real property, based on her Petition. Before the court is Johnson's Motion to Expunge Notice of Pendency of (Lis Pendens). The court, on its own motion, takes judicial notice of the record in this case. The motion to expunge the lis pendens is DENIED. "At any time after notice of pendency of action has been recorded, any party, or any nonparty with an interest in the real property affected thereby, may apply to the court in which the action is pending to expunge the notice. [ ] Evidence or declarations may be filed with the motion to expunge the notice. [ ] The claimant shall have the burden of proof under Sections 405.31 and 405.32." (Code of Civil Procedure section 405.30.) The court shall order the notice expunged if the court finds either that the pleading on which the notice is based does not contain a real property claim, or that the claimant has not established by a preponderance of the evidence the probable validity of the real property claim. (Code of Civil Procedure sections 405.31, 405.32; see J&A Mash & Barrel, LLC v. Superior Court (2022) 74 Cal. App. 5th 1, 22 ["The only substantive grounds upon which a lis pendens can be expunged is if the underlying action does not contain a real property claim or the real property claim lacks probable validity."].) A claim has "probable validity" where it is more likely than not that the plaintiff will obtain a judgment against the defendant on that claim. (Code of Civil Procedure section 481.190.) A "real property claim" requires a pleading to (1) identify specific real property that is the subject of the action, and (2) show that a cause or causes of action would affect title to or right to possession of the specific property. (Gale v. Superior Court (2004) 122 CA4th 1388, 1396.) Here, the parties do not dispute that the Petition identifies a specific real property that is the subject of the action - 163 Beverly Street- and that the Petition seeks an order under Probate Code section 850 et seq determining the real property to be an asset of the Trust and not an estate asset. The pleading on which the lis pendens is based contains a real property claim. [Part 1 of 6]

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE HERMAN F. JOHNSON, SR. REVOCABLE 2010 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion To Expunge Notice Of Pendency
rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 6] As for the required element that the settlor be competent, there is a "rebuttable presumption affecting the burden of proof that all persons have the capacity to make decisions and to be responsible for their acts or decisions." (Probate Code section 810.) The party challenging that presumption bears the burden of proof. A determination that a person lacks "the legal capacity to perform a specific act should be based on evidence of a deficit in one or more of the person's mental functions rather than on a diagnosis of a person's mental or physical disorder." (Probate Code section 810). A person has capacity to make a decision if the person has the ability to communicate the decision and to understand and appreciate "[t]he rights, duties, and responsibilities created by, or affected by the decision[;] [t]he probable consequences for the decisionmaker and, where appropriate, the persons affected by the decision[;] [t]he significant risks, benefits, and reasonable alternatives involved in the decision." (Probate Code section 812). The parties agree that the Trust was executed while their father was in his final illness. (Robinson Decl. at par. 7; Motion at 2:24-28.) Their father's poor physical health is not alone enough to rebut the presumption of legal capacity, however. There is no showing on the record currently before the court that Settlor was deficient in one or more metal function at the time he executed the Trust. Robinson's evidence shows Settlor communicated his testamentary intent to Robinson, and reviewed and approved the Trust document Robinson drafted. (Robinson Decl. at par. 6, 7.) There is no showing on the record before the court that Settlor was unable to communicate his decision to establish the 2010 Trust and designate the trust property and beneficiaries, or to appreciate and understand the consequences of those decisions. 1. Trust Void Due to Donative Transfer Under Probate Code section 21380 Johnson's reliance on the presumptions set out in Probate Code section 21380 is misplaced; those presumptions do not apply where, as here, the challenged donative transfer is to the settlor's daughter. (See Prob. Code section 21382, subd.(a).) 2. Trust Invalid Due to Undue Influence Johnson alleges that 2010 Trust is invalid because it was procured by undue influence, as defined by Welfare & Institutions Code section 15610.70 and Probate Code section 86. A person challenging the validity of a trust instrument on the grounds that the trustor lacked capacity to execute the document or did so under the undue influence of another carries the burden of proof on those allegations. (Doolittle v. Exchange Bank (2015) 241 Cal.App.4th 529, 545.) Under Welfare & Institutions Code section 15610.70 and Probate Code section 86, "'Undue influence' means excessive persuasion that causes another person to act or refrain from acting by overcoming that person's free will and results in inequity." There is no fixed formula for determining undue influence. (Beckmann v. Beckmann (1959)174 Cal. App. 2d 717, 721.) In making that determination, the following elements must be considered: (1) the vulnerability of the victim; (2) the influencer's apparent authority; (3) the actions or tactics used by the influencer, such as controlling necessaries of life, medication, the victim's interactions with others, access to information, or sleep, use of affection, intimidation, or coercion, initiation of changes in personal or property rights, use of haste or secrecy in effecting those changes; and (4) the equity of the result. Evidence of an inequitable result, without more, is not sufficient to prove undue influence. (Welfare & Institutions Code section 15610.70.) [Part 3 of 6]

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE HERMAN F. JOHNSON, SR. REVOCABLE 2010 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion To Expunge Notice Of Pendency
rulings.Rulings: [Part 6 of 6] Under Code of Civil Procedure section 405.38, "The court shall direct that the party prevailing on any motion under this chapter be awarded the reasonable attorney's fees and costs of making or opposing the motion unless the court finds that the other party acted with substantial justification or that other circumstances make the imposition of attorney's fees and costs unjust." The court finds that Johnson acted with substantial justification in that the need for, or appropriateness of, this lis pendens is far from immediately apparent. After all, in anyone were to try to sell the property it would be Robinson, not Johnson; he has no access that would allow him to sell the real property. Further, Robinson was content with status quo for more than a decade, doing nothing to effectuate Settlor's intent or transfer the real property as required by the Trust, and now appears to be acting only because of her brother's legal action to settle title. The circumstances beg the question why now the lis pendens? Finally, the court finds that other circumstances make the imposition of attorney's fees and costs unjust. Petitioner shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling as published above. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on September 13, 2022. Remote appearance is available consistent with San Francisco Superior Court Local Rules, rule 14.1(D). To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-1-669-254-5252 and key in meeting ID "160 225 4765#", then participant ID "0#", then password "514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204. [Part 6 of 6]

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE HERMAN F. JOHNSON, SR. REVOCABLE 2010 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion To Expunge Notice Of Pendency
rulings.Rulings: [Part 4 of 6] Undue influence, in the context of a testamentary disposition of property by will or trust, is "pressure brought to bear directly on the testamentary act, sufficient to overcome the testator's free will, amounting in effect to coercion destroying the testator's free agency." (Rice v. Clark (2002) 28 Cal.4th 89, 96.) The determination of whether undue influence was exerted to obtain a certain testamentary disposition is, ultimately, a factual inquiry. The record currently before the court shows that Robinson was in a confidential relationship with Settlor at the time the Trust was executed, as his daughter, informal caretaker, and his named agent under Advance Health Care Directive and Durable Power of Attorney Decedent dated July 30, 2010 (Robinson Decl. par. 2, 4, Exh. 1). As discussed above, there is a showing that Settlor was physically in poor health when he executed the Trust, but there is an insufficient showing on the record currently before the court that he was legally incapacitated or otherwise unable to make reasoned decisions. There is no showing in the record currently before the court that Robinson used actions or tactics to influence the Decedent's decision to establish the Trust, set its terms, or obtain his approval of the document. (Robinson Decl. par. 6, 7.) Rather, Robinson's evidence shows that Settlor initiated the process of establishing the Trust, and asked Robinson to help him effectuate his intent. (Robinson Decl. par. 6, 7.) The Trust does not distribute assets equally between Decedent's children, Johnson and Robinson, but names only Decedent's daughter and grandchildren as beneficiaries. While a factual element to consider, that alone is not enough to prove Robinson used undue influence to bring about Trust. Cumulatively, the record currently before the court on this motion to expunge lis pendens does not rise to support a determination of undue influence. 3. Trust Invalid Due to Elder Abuse Johnson also alleges the 2010 Trust is void because Robinson committed elder abuse, both in obtaining it via undue influence, and in "taking secreting and retaining the Trust," citing Welfare & Institutions Code sections 15610.30 and 15657.6. (See also, Objector's Separate Case Management Stmnt. at 10:11-14.) "Financial Elder Abuse" under Welfare & Institutions Code section 15610.30 occurs when a person takes, secretes, appropriates, obtains, or retains real or personal property of an elder or dependent adult for a wrongful use, or with intent to defraud, or by undue influence. As discussed above, there is insufficient showing before the court on the current record to support a determination that the 2010 Trust was obtained via undue influence. [Part 4 of 6]

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE HERMAN F. JOHNSON, SR. REVOCABLE 2010 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion To Expunge Notice Of Pendency
rulings.Rulings: [5 of 6] Johnson's implication in the moving papers that Robinson took property from their father with "intent to defraud" an elder or dependent adult is otherwise unclear. The elements of fraud are: (1) a representation, (2) that is false, (3) made with knowledge of its falsity, and (4) with an intent to deceive, coupled with (5) actual detrimental reliance, and (6) resulting damage. There are no clear allegations in the papers that Robinson perpetrated fraud on their father by making in a knowingly false statement on which their father relied to his detriment and resulting damage. Johnson's Separate Case Management Statement, under the heading "Petitioner committed Elder Financial Abuse in this case," appears to fault Robinson for not hiring an attorney for their father, to review the Trust document with him (at 7:13-17, 10: 23-11:4). Otherwise, the alleged fraud or intent to deceive appears to spring from Robinson not disclosing the existence of the Trust to Johnson or other family members, and Robinson causing the Trust to be executed in order to alter their father's 1977 Will and disinherit Johnson, in favor her interests and the interests of her children. (see, Motion at 3:21-26, Objector's Separate Case Management Stmnt. at 3:2-5, 10:3-8, 10:11-14.) This does not go to a showing of financial elder abuse of Decedent. Welfare & Institutions Code section 15657.6 is not applicable on the record currently before the court. Section 15657.6 pertains to the remedies available when the wrongful taking of real or personal property, is done to an elder or dependent adult who either (1) lacks capacity pursuant to Section 812 of the Probate Code, or (2) is of "unsound mind," pursuant to Section 39 of the Civil Code. There is no showing on the record currently before the court that Decedent lacked capacity pursuant to Probate Code section 812, or was of "unsound mind" per Civil Code section 39, when he executed the 2010 Trust. Johnson's evidentiary showing does not establish by a preponderance of the evidence that the Trust instrument is the result of undue influence or fraud. Nor does it otherwise substantially undermine Robinson's showing that the Trust is legitimate. At best, Johnson relies on inferences from their father's age and illness, Robinson's close relationship with their father and her involvement in drafting the Trust. (See, e.g., Motion at 4:14-18, Reply at 2:18-28, 7:18-24.) On this record, those inferences yield to Robinson's substantial showing that the Trust reflects Settlor's intent. Johnson has submitted evidence that, if admissible (an issue the court is not deciding at this time), a reasonable finder of fact could possibly base a finding of undue influence and/or elder abuse. However, on the record currently before the court, Robinson's evidence predominates. Robinson's demand for fees and costs is DENIED. [Part 5 of 6]

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE HERMAN F. JOHNSON, SR. REVOCABLE 2010 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion To Expunge Notice Of Pendency
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 6] The court now turns to Johnson's contention that Robinson's real property claim lacks probable validity. Johnson argues the Trust is invalid as the product of: (1) undue influence or fraud, by way of the presumption of Probate Code section 21380; (2) undue influence as defined by Welfare & Institutions Code section 15610.70 and Probate Code section 86; and/or (3) elder abuse as defined under Welfare & Institutions Code sections 15610.30 and 15657.6. (See Reply 2:7-10; Reply 2:17-19; Objector's Separate Case Management Stmnt. at 8:13-18, 9:5-12, 10:11-14 Petitioner's Case Management Stmnt, at 8:14-10:11.) The burden is on Robinson to "establish by a preponderance of the evidence the probable validity of the real property claim." Johnson is correct that the legislative intent of the lis pendens statutory scheme "was to make the standard on a motion to expunge a notice of lis pendens equivalent to the probable validity standard applicable under the attachment law (Code Civil Procedure section 484.090) [citations]. That is, the plaintiff must "at least establish a prima facie case. If the defendant makes an appearance, the court must then consider the relative merits of the positions of the respective parties and make a determination of the probable outcome of the litigation." (Howard S. Wright Constr. Co. v. Superior Ct. (2003) 106 Cal. App. 4th 314, 319-20, quoting Cal. Law Revision Comm. coms. to Code Civil Procedure section 481.190, definition of "probable validity" in attachment actions.) Here, Robinson has established by a preponderance of the evidence the probable validity of the claim that the Trust is valid and 163 Beverly Street is a Trust asset. The five elements required to create a valid express trust are (1) a competent trustor, (2) trust intent, (3) trust property, (4) trust purpose, and (5) a beneficiary. (See Probate Code section 15200 et seq). Robinson's evidence shows: Settlor manifested an intention to create a trust and asked Robinson to help him effectuate that intent (Robinson Decl. at par. 6, 7). Robinson sought legal advice from "a legal clinic at UC Hastings" and drafted the Trust based on that advice. (Robinson Decl. at par. 7.) Robinson's Separate Case Management Statement clarifies, "UC Hastings has a Legal Partnership for Seniors (MLPS) Clinic. The clinic partners with UCSF and the San Francisco VA Medical Center (where Settlor was in the hospital), to provide advance health care, estate planning, and other wrap-around legal services to older adult patients." (Petitioner's Separate Case Management Stmnt. at 10:3-7.) Settlor reviewed the document Robinson drafted and approved it. (Robinson Decl. at par. 7.) Robinson then hired a notary and Settlor executed the Trust. (Robinson Decl. at par. 7). Settlor identified 163 Beverly Street as a Trust asset, both in the body of the Trust and on Schedule A of the Trust, and named Robinson and her two children - Settlor's daughter and grandchildren - as beneficiaries. (Trust, paragraph 1 and Schedule A.) Settlor's signature on the Trust document is notarized, verifying the identity of the person who signed the documents. [Part 2 of 6]

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF EDWARD J. GIETZEN
rulings.Calendar Matter: PETITION FOR PROBATE OF WILL AND FOR LETTERS TESTAMENTARY
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 11/7/22. No earlier date available. No response yet as of 9/9/22 to the issues posted in the tentative ruling for 6/6/22. Copies of UNFILED papers were delivered to the examiner. Un-endorsed filed copies will not be reviewed. [LRSF 14.7 C.] If an amended petition is actually filed, courtesy copies of it and all supporting documents must be received by the assigned examiner by no later than 10/27/22. An amending pleading requires the same notice as the pleading it amends, including publication. [CRC 7.53(a).] Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10.B.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF KRISTINE B. MORGAN
rulings.Calendar Matter: PETITION FOR PROBATE OF WILL AND FOR LETTERS TESTAMENTARY
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 11/7/22. (No earlier availability.) Issue(s) necessitating continuance: A Judicial Council form must not be used for a supplement to a pleading. [CRC 7.104(b).] A supplement to a Petition for Probate must be prepared on pleading paper and must be fully verified (including the date of the verification). Notice was sent to Phoebe Taylor Morgan at an address that differs from the one set forth in item 8 of the petition without any explanation. Does "beneficiary" next to the individual's name mean he/she is a TRUST beneficiary? (There was no need to publish notice twice. [Probate Code Section 1205.] No courtesy copy of either proof of publication has been delivered yet to the examiner. [LRSF 14.7 B. and C.]) Courtesy copies of a proper and fully verified supplement to the petition addressing these issues must be received by the assigned Examiner by no later than 10/27/22. Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10 B.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF DAVID GOLDMAN
rulings.Calendar Matter: PETITION FOR PROBATE OF WILL AND FOR LETTERS TESTAMENTARY
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 11/7/22. (No earlier availability.) Issue(s) necessitating continuance: The amended petition file stamped 8/15/22 was not actually e-filed until 8/17/22, when it was first available for viewing. Notice may not be mailed before the filing (e-filing) of the pleading requiring notice. [LRSF 14.14 A.] The notice did not indicate that an AMENDED petition seeking to admit a LOST will has been filed. The proof of publication filed on 8/26/22 (no courtesy copy yet) does not indicate that an AMENDED petition will be heard. (Why is the surviving spouse not seeking relief via a Spousal Property Petition? [Probate Code Sect. 13502. CRC 7.652(b). LRSF 14.35 I.5.]) Courtesy copies of any papers filed to address these issues (correct proof of publication and proof of service of the correct Notice of Amended Petition for Probate of Lost Will) must be received by the assigned Examiner by no later than 10/27/22. Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10 B.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF THOMAS RUTHERFORD JACKSON
rulings.Calendar Matter: PETITION FOR LETTERS OF ADMINISTRATION
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 10/12/2022. Examiner continuances are final, not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10 B. 1. Petitioner must submit a hard copy of the proposed order to the Examiner in room 202 at least five (5) court days in advance of the scheduled hearing date. [LRSF 14.7]. Proposed orders dropped off without a hearing date or examiner name on the envelope or a cover page, will be processed by the clerks as time allows and may cause delays. 2. A proposed nonresident personal representative will be required to post a bond to protect California creditors, even if the will waives, or all beneficiaries waive, bond. A declaration or attachment to the petition setting forth in detail the anticipated liabilities of the decedent and claims against the estate will be used by the Court to determine the amount of the bond, but in no event will the bond be less than $20,000. (LRSF 14.16 F.) 3. Petitioner must file and give notice on the mandatory judicial council form DE-121 that complies with Local Rule 14.1. D. 4. Page 4 of the petition under #8 does not state the age of the persons listed and the Decedent's parents and their date of death are not listed. An endorsed-filed single sided hard copy of all documents filed in response to these notes, as well as any other documents required must be received by this Examiner in room 202 no later than 4 pm on 9/26/2022 [LRSF 14.7 B.]
